contract.note: This is an MCD assessment: a projection of a scan onto the malicious-code question. It recommends a disposition (a next-action), not a maliciousness verdict. Severity (how bad if real) is reported separately from confidence (how sure), and every finding states what would disprove it. The rules own coverage and the mcd lens owns judgment; this view composes them and does not re-analyze.

correlations.corroboration: 2 signal type(s) (package, source) co-located. Co-location across independent signals is corroborating context; it does NOT alter any individual finding's confidence. A disposition may weigh it through an explicit, stated rule.
disproof: The findings sit in the same file but are not actually connected in control or data flow (co-location only); a reachability check that finds no path breaks the link.
Each member finding's own disproof criteria still apply; disprove the members and the correlation dissolves.
insights: the package's install step runs code that downloads and executes a remote payload, so the dropper fires at install time, before any explicit use
correlations.narrative: 2 malicious-code findings co-occur in `setup.py`: BP-DROPPER, BP-SUPPLY.

notes: Enrichment providers: local static analysis only; registry/OSINT/threat-intel providers are offline, so confidence reflects static evidence without external corroboration. Offline absence lowers certainty; it is not a clean bill of health.

Implemented MCD composition coverage: BP-SUPPLY, BP-TYPOSQUAT, BP-DROPPER, BP-CREDTHEFT, BP-OBFEXEC, BP-BACKDOOR, BP-EXFIL, BP-RANSOM, BP-TIMEBOMB, BP-MINER, BP-ROOTKIT, BP-WORM, BP-TROJAN, BP-AGENTMANIP, BP-LATERAL, BP-MITM. CLEAR means no findings matched this implemented set under the current static methods; it is not a full safety verdict and does not include runtime behavior or unavailable enrichment.

drivers: 1 cross-signal correlated cluster(s) at high/critical severity (corroborated co-location)
disposition.rationale: Recommend quarantine: 3 high/critical-severity finding(s) at proof-aware confidence >= 0.65; 1 cross-signal correlated cluster(s) at high/critical severity (corroborated co-location). Quarantine means hold the artifact and do not install or run it until the verification questions are answered. This is a recommended next-action from the evidence, not a maliciousness verdict; severity and confidence are reported separately.
disposition.thresholds: Disposition rule (deterministic; severity and confidence kept separate): quarantine if any high/critical-severity finding has proof-aware confidence >= 0.65, or a cross-signal correlated cluster is high/critical severity. Findings whose proof depth is only same-file co-occurrence or co-location are not promoted to quarantine by enrichment alone. Review if mcd findings exist but do not meet that bar; clear if there are no mcd findings. The model never sets the disposition.

executiveSummary.author: engine
executiveSummary.text: Disposition: QUARANTINE. 3 malicious-code finding(s); highest severity high, highest confidence 0.9 (severity and confidence are independent). Strongest correlation: 2 malicious-code findings co-occur in `setup.py`: BP-DROPPER, BP-SUPPLY. Verify next: (1) What does the install hook fetch, decode, or execute, and from where? (2) Is the destination maintainer-controlled and documented? (3) Does behavior differ under CI/sandbox?

amplifiers: Behavior triggers at install, not at explicit use.
findings.claim: A package install hook (npm lifecycle script or Python setup.py) runs network, shell, or decode-and-execute behavior before any user code is invoked.
disproofCriteria: The install code is a documented, reproducible build step.
Fetched/decoded content is version-pinned with integrity (hash/signature) checks.

actions: Block auto-install in CI until reviewed
response.summary: Engineering referral + passive monitoring until verified.

findings.title: Install-time payload path

verification.question: What does the install hook fetch, decode, or execute, and from where?
verification.reason: Confirm the payload behavior and destination.

verification.question: Is the destination maintainer-controlled and documented?
Does behavior differ under CI/sandbox?
verification.reason: Install-time gating is a strong malicious signal.

amplifiers: Proof depth: proven intra-file taint - fetch -> exec via variable `fetch` at line 4.
findings.claim: Intra-file dataflow traces remote-fetched content into a code-execution sink (exec/eval/shell) in this file — the download-and-execute dropper shape, proven by a connected value path rather than mere co-occurrence. Dataflow: PROVEN (fetch -> exec); the fetch value reaches the exec via variable `fetch`, a connected path rather than mere co-occurrence.
disproofCriteria: The traced value is inert data (config/text), not code, and the sink treats it as data.
The fetched source is fixed, documented, and integrity-checked before it reaches the sink.

actions: Pin/vendor the payload with integrity checks
response.summary: Block or sandbox until the fetched payload and integrity story are verified.

findings.title: Download-and-execute (dataflow-proven dropper)

verification.question: What is fetched and then executed, and who controls it?
verification.reason: Remote control of the executed bytes is the key risk.

verification.question: Is the fetched payload pinned by hash/signature before execution?
verification.reason: Integrity verification can disprove the malicious shape.

amplifiers: Remote content is executed directly rather than written and inspected first.
findings.claim: Code fetches remote content and passes it directly into a shell or dynamic evaluator. That is the direct remote-execution variant of the dropper shape; no intermediate file write is required.
disproofCriteria: The downloaded content is fixed, maintainer-controlled, documented, and integrity-checked before execution.
The shell/eval sink receives only a trusted local constant, not network content.
No control-flow or dataflow path links these observations; they co-occur in one file or component but are not reachable from one another.

actions: Do not pipe remote content to a shell
Vendor or pin the script with integrity checks
response.summary: Block or sandbox until the remote payload and integrity story are verified.

findings.title: Download-and-execute (direct remote exec)

verification.question: What remote content is executed and who controls it?
verification.reason: Remote control of the executed bytes is the key risk.

verification.question: Is the downloaded script pinned by hash/signature before execution?
verification.reason: Integrity verification can disprove the malicious shape.

evidence.summary: setup.py executes code at install time
